title: Build Housing with Care Act of 2025
short_title: Build Housing with Care Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Build Housing with Care Act of 2025 creates a new program that gives money to help build housing near child care centers. This means families could live close to where their children get care during the day. The bill also asks the government to study how well families in public housing can find and pay for child care. The goal is to make it easier for parents to find both a place to live and child care in the same area.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Build Housing with Care Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Purpose
sections.text: The goal of this law is to make it easier for people to find affordable housing and child care. It does this by creating a grant program that encourages putting housing and child care services in the same location or nearby locations.

sections.title: Section 3 Housing and Child Care Provider Co-location Grant Program
sections.text: The Secretary of Housing and Urban Development will create a program to give grants to qualifying organizations. These grants will help pay for designing, planning, building, converting, fixing up, preserving, or renovating buildings that combine housing with child care services. When developing this program, the Secretary must work with the Secretary of Health and Human Services through the Assistant Secretary of the Administration for Children and Families, the Secretary of the Treasury through the Director of the Community Development Financial Institutions Fund, and the Secretary of Agriculture through the Under Secretary for Rural Development. To get a grant, organizations must apply and include several things in their application. They must prove that their child care provider can receive vouchers or assistance under the Child Care and Development Block Grant Act of nineteen ninety. If they are building a new facility or plan to give money to other organizations, they must promise to partner with a qualified child care provider within one year of getting the money. They must also promise not to evict residents from housing connected to their project. They must describe how they will tell residents about their plans and get them involved. They must show they follow all required federal, state, and local environmental laws and land use rules, unless they are using the grant money just for planning or design, or unless their project does not need environmental review. They must include a business plan for their child care provider that shows their budget, their license or application for a license, and any contracts they have with government agencies. When giving out grants, the Secretary will give priority to organizations whose child care providers will operate in child care deserts (areas with much more need for child care than available spots), low income communities, or rural areas. Priority also goes to providers that are Head Start providers or serve at least ten percent of children from very low income families, or that partner with community development financial institutions. Each organization can receive up to ten million dollars. Organizations can only use grant money for designing, planning, building, buying, preserving, converting, fixing up, long term leasing, or renovating facilities that combine housing with child care. Organizations that get grants can give some of the money to government agencies, nonprofit housing developers, public housing agencies, tribal housing organizations, or other appropriate groups to carry out these activities. Community development financial institutions can use grant money to create financial products like loans for these projects. Organizations can use up to ten percent of their grant money for early planning and design work for new facilities. They can also use up to ten percent to work with community development financial institutions that provide technical help and training, help home based child care providers meet licensing standards, and do early development work. The Secretary will provide technical help and publish best practices online to help these combined facilities operate well. Starting one year after this law passes and then every year while the program runs, the Secretary must report to Congress about how the program is working. The report must include how many grants were given, what activities were funded, how many child care spots were created including spots for low income children and children learning English as a second language, how many child care spots were saved from closing, how many residents use or work for the child care program, how many people work for the child care providers, information about who lives in the housing, how many projects were helped through different types of funding, how many early childhood providers got help, and what types of organizations received grants. Congress approves spending one hundred million dollars each year from twenty twenty five through twenty thirty for this program. This section defines important terms. A caregiver is defined the same way as in the Child Care and Development Block Grant Act. An eligible child care provider is also defined the same way as in that Act. A child care desert is an area that has at least three times more children than the licensed child care providers can serve, or an area with no licensed child care providers. A co location facility is housing that has a child care provider inside, on the same property, or nearby that serves the people who live in that housing. A community development financial institution is defined the same way as in the Community Development Banking and Financial Institutions Act of nineteen ninety four. Community development corporations and community housing development organizations are defined the same way as in the Cranston Gonzalez National Affordable Housing Act. An eligible entity means organizations that can apply for grants including community development financial institutions, child care providers, public housing authorities, government agencies, Indian tribes and tribal organizations, community development corporations, housing developers using low income housing tax credits or new market tax credits, nonprofit housing developers, community housing development organizations, groups of two or more of these types of organizations working together, or other organizations the Secretary decides are appropriate. Indian tribe and tribal organization are defined the same way as in the Indian Self Determination and Education Assistance Act and include tribal housing organizations and groups that serve Native Hawaiians. Low income family, public housing agency, and very low income family are all defined the same way as in the United States Housing Act of nineteen thirty seven.

sections.title: Section 4 GAO Study and Report Regarding Child Care Access for Residents of Public Housing
sections.text: The Comptroller General of the United States must study how available and affordable child care is for people who live in public housing. The study must describe how money from various programs like Community Development Block Grant funds, New Market Tax Credits, Community Development Financial Institution Program funds, Low Income Housing Tax Credits, Capital Management Fund funds, and HOME Investment Partnerships Program funds have been used by child care providers to build, renovate, or improve their facilities. The study must look at how housing and child care costs affect the financial future of public housing residents. It must find out what percentage of public housing residents both spend too much of their income on housing costs and spend at least seven percent of their household income on child care. The study must identify state and local laws that make it hard to build or maintain child care facilities in or near public housing. It must look at how rental assistance under section eight of the United States Housing Act affects public housing residents ability to afford child care and other basic needs like food, internet and phone service, and transportation like cars, bikes, or public transit. The study must evaluate how well tax credits and programs like the Child and Dependent Care Tax Credit, Earned Income Tax Credit, Child Tax Credit, and Dependent Care Flexible Spending Account work for public housing residents. This includes looking at how much people know about these programs, how well outreach and education about them works, and whether each program provides enough help to cover child care costs. The study must look at how much information public housing residents get about child care resources from federal agencies or public housing agencies. Finally, the study must make recommendations for improving access to child care in and near public housing and for helping people learn about federal programs that help with housing and child care costs. The Comptroller General must submit a report about this study to Congress within twelve months after this law passes. The report goes to the Committees on Financial Services and Education and Workforce in the House of Representatives and the Committees on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs and Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions in the Senate. This section defines eligible child care provider the same way as in the Child Care and Development Block Grant Act, public housing dwelling unit as housing helped by the public housing program under the United States Housing Act of nineteen thirty seven, and public housing agency the same way as in the United States Housing Act of nineteen thirty seven.
